diag: Both herbs are lush and well-coloured — glossy upright basil, dense compact oregano.
action: Pinch basil tips often and snip off any flower buds to keep it leafy. Oregano likes it drier, so water once the soil surface feels dry.

diag: Several leaves have brown scorched patches and are curling — classic sun scorch from sudden direct sun and heat, or drying out too far.
action: Move to bright, indirect light or give afternoon shade, and re-introduce sun slowly. Water when the top 2–3 cm dries; avoid swinging between bone-dry and soaked. Trim the worst leaves.

diag: Strong upright canes and healthy red-edged foliage. The little lower-leaf yellowing is normal shedding.
action: Bright light is fine. Let the top few cm dry between waterings. If tips brown, switch to filtered or rain water — it dislikes salts and fluoride.

diag: Flowering well but getting leggy and sprawling, with some spent blooms. The shallow dish also dries out fast.
action: Deadhead spent flowers and pinch leggy stems back by a third for bushier growth and more blooms. Feed every 1–2 weeks and check water daily in the heat.

diag: Big, glossy, evenly green leaves with no real blemishes — this one is happy.
action: Keep it in the same bright spot (they hate being moved). Water when the top 3–5 cm dries, dust the leaves, and rotate occasionally for even growth.

diag: Full, bushy, and brightly variegated — good light is keeping the cream-and-green pattern strong.
action: Keep the bright light to hold the variegation. Water when the top dries, prune to shape if it gets tall, and check leaf undersides for scale or mites.

diag: Healthy upright paddles with only minor tip browning — establishing well for a young plant.
action: Full sun and deep watering when the top few cm dry. Feed through the warm season. Tip browning is usually salt build-up — flush the pot occasionally.

diag: Glossy, deep-green leaflets radiating cleanly from each stalk. Looks vigorous.
action: Bright, indirect light; water when the top 2–3 cm dries. It drops leaves if overwatered, so let it dry a touch between drinks.

diag: Dense, vivid, healthy growth right across the bowl — clearly thriving.
action: Keep the soil consistently moist and harvest often. Mint will eventually crowd the verbena, so consider giving the lemon verbena its own pot.

diag: Vigorous young growth, but the orange-brown flecks on the leaves point to early rust or sun/heat spotting.
action: Remove spotted leaves, water at the base (keep foliage dry), and improve airflow. Give full sun and add support as they climb. If spots spread, treat as bean rust.

diag: Generally healthy and full, with some browning on the lower frond tips.
action: Keep the soil evenly moist (not soggy) and out of harsh midday sun. Brown tips come from dry air, underwatering, or salts — water consistently and snip the brown tips.

diag: Strong central rosette and firm, upright leaves. A very easy, happy plant.
action: Lots of sun and little fuss. Let the soil dry out well between waterings — overwatering is the main risk. Use a well-draining mix.

diag: Trailing nicely, but several leaves are bleached white and scorched — too much direct sun for a variegated Hoya.
action: Move to bright, indirect light to stop the bleaching. The thick leaves store water, so water only when the soil dries. Remove badly burnt leaves and let it trail or climb.

diag: Pale, greyish colour with some tip browning — mild stress, most likely intense sun plus a watering imbalance.
action: Acclimatise to strong sun gradually and water deeply only when the soil is fully dry. Use a gritty, free-draining mix and make sure the pot drains, to avoid rot.

diag: Widespread brown necrosis and dieback on the leaves — a sign of underwatering or heat scorch, possibly with salt build-up or root stress.
action: Check drainage, then water deeply and consistently. Move to bright light with some afternoon shade until it recovers, flush the soil to clear salts, and remove dead leaves before feeding.

diag: Deep, healthy leaves with red new growth at the tips — a good sign it has the right light and water balance.
action: Keep the bright light, water when the top few cm dries, and wipe the leaves. Stake the main stem if it starts to lean.

diag: Leggy, sparse and woody with drying foliage. Lavender hates damp roots and rich soil, and this one is stressed.
action: Move to a gritty, sharply-draining pot in full sun and water only when dry. Prune back by up to a third after flowering (never into bare old wood) and go easy on feeding.
